the dangers and loneliness became

Only few members of that old
crowd still linger to tell of the tor
turous fight with the. winter ele
ments and of the fate of luckless
comrades. Will Smith is one of
them. He was in charge of one of
the snow bucking crews which
worked up and down the switch
back about Wellington and Scenic,
over the very spot in which the


two Great Northern trains were


buried with all on board the other
day. Smith is division superintend-
ent up at Kalispell, Mont, now. He
has gained position in reward for
services rendered.


A TERRIBLE DEATH ON RUN
AWAY TRAIN.

ern engine is about the only sur

Dahlgren met the terrible fate


that is momentarily expected up
there in the hills. Part of his train
of 40 cars broke away from the
engine that was toiling up the hill


with them. and. with Dahlgren smil-
ing and waving his last farewell to
old familiar comrades, standing
white, almost petrified with horror
at Wellington station, he plunged
with his train, running a hundred
miles an hour, down the steep grade
off Windy Arm. His mangled body


was found weeks later under the
hideous heap of shattered timbers
and twisted iron remairYy Of his
40 cars. His train actually doubled
up into a stack less than 100 feet


in length in the canyon.


TWO COMRADES DIED ON SAME
JO6.


DAILY died some months ago after
his legs had been severed In a


wreck of the Owl train. running
between Seattle and Vancouver,


and Lanahan, his old comrade, who
took his place. met a similar fate


within 30 days afterward.
